candidates.primary_principle: Calibrate user trust with explicit explanations of what the AI can/cannot do; expose data practices so humans can audit, correct, and exit.
candidates.citation_form: Google PAIR. People + AI Guidebook. pair.withgoogle.com/guidebook.
candidates.extracted_excerpt: A toolkit for teams building human-centered AI products.

candidates.primary_principle: Operationalize fairness, reliability, safety, inclusiveness, transparency, and accountability as concrete product requirements with reviewable artifacts.
candidates.citation_form: Microsoft (2022). Responsible AI Standard v2. microsoft.com/ai/responsible-ai.
candidates.extracted_excerpt: Explore how Microsoft is committed to responsible AI practices and advancing AI technology with ethical principles.

candidates.primary_principle: Be helpful AND honest AND non-manipulative AND respectful of user autonomy; resolve conflicts via documented hierarchy, not implicit defaults.
AI should be socially beneficial, avoid creating unfair bias, be built and tested for safety, and be accountable to people.
candidates.extracted_excerpt: We approach this work together, by collaborating with a broad range of partners to make breakthroughs and maximize the broad benefits of AI, while empowering others to build their own bold and responsible solutions.

candidates.source_name: Constitutional AI: Harmlessness from AI Feedback
candidates.primary_principle: Use explicit principles and AI feedback to reduce harmful assistant behavior without relying only on human preference labels.
candidates.citation_form: Bai, Y. et al. (2022). Constitutional AI: Harmlessness from AI Feedback. arXiv:2212.08073.
candidates.extracted_excerpt: Abstract page for arXiv paper 2212.08073: Constitutional AI: Harmlessness from AI Feedback

candidates.primary_principle: Optimize language models directly against preference data without a separate reinforcement-learning loop when the objective allows it.
candidates.citation_form: Rafailov, R. et al. (2023). Direct Preference Optimization. arXiv:2305.18290.
candidates.extracted_excerpt: Abstract page for arXiv paper 2305.18290: Direct Preference Optimization: Your Language Model is Secretly a Reward Model
